mappings.rationale: Directly relevant to subsection B.2.e on the Classical period of Chinese literature, and to Han and T'ang cultural developments in prose writing, poetry, and literary arts.
Directly relevant to subsection B.2.e on Confucianism as a major school of Chinese philosophy, and to its role in Han educational developments and later periods.
Relevant to the section's discussion of the introduction of Buddhism during the Han period and the spread of Buddhism during the Six Dynasties period.
The section opens with a discussion of historiographic problems in studying Chinese civilization and the documentary and archaeological historical sources.
The section discusses archaeological sources extensively, including Neolithic pottery cultures (Yang-shao, Lung-shan), the Shang site at Anyang, and Bronze Age developments.
Relevant to the section's discussion of the development of Chinese script in the Shang period and the invention of paper during the Han dynasty.
Relevant to the section's coverage of Han expansion into Central Asia, Pan Ch'ao's campaigns, and the cultural exchanges between China and the wider world.
Relevant to the section's discussion of the construction of the Great Wall under Shih Huang Ti, military campaigns of the Han and T'ang dynasties, and Chinese military developments.
Relevant to the section's coverage of successive Chinese dynasties — Hsia, Shang, Chou, Ch'in, Han, Sui, and T'ang — and their dynastic succession patterns.
